federal_credit.expiration_note: The 30% federal solar tax credit (ITC) is available through 2032. It drops to 26% in 2033 and 22% in 2034.
federal_credit.message: The 30% Federal Solar Tax Credit is available NOW through 2032. Lock in maximum savings before the step-down begins.

state_alerts.message: California's NEM 3.0 significantly reduced export compensation rates by approximately 75%. Existing systems are grandfathered under previous rates. Battery storage is now essential for maximum savings.
New York's Value of Distributed Energy Resources (VDER) tariff is replacing traditional net metering. Existing installations are grandfathered. NY-Sun incentives are still available but declining.
Arizona state solar tax credit of 25% (up to $1,000) is available through 2028. APS and SRP export compensation rates continue to decline. Act now for best rates.
Indiana net metering is being phased down to an avoided-cost rate structure. New solar customers will receive reduced export compensation. Lock in current rates before further reductions take effect.
DTE Energy and Consumers Energy have reached net metering capacity caps. New solar customers are placed on distributed generation tariffs with lower export compensation rates.
Hawaii offers the highest state tax credit in the nation at 35% of system cost, combined with the highest electricity rates. Solar plus battery storage delivers the fastest payback period in the US.
South Carolina 25% state solar tax credit has no cap, making it one of the most generous state credits in the nation. Combined with the 30% federal ITC, homeowners can offset 55% of system costs.
Massachusetts SMART program incentive rates are declining as capacity blocks fill up. Earlier adopters receive higher rates locked in for 10 years. SREC-II market remains strong but competitive.

New Jersey Successor Solar Incentive (SuSI) TREC values remain among the strongest in the nation. Combined with full retail net metering and sales/property tax exemptions, NJ is one of the best states for solar.
Utah is transitioning from net metering to net billing under Rocky Mountain Power. Export credit rates are declining annually. Earlier adopters benefit from higher grandfather rates.

utility_alerts.message: PG&E NEM 3.0 significantly reduces export compensation to approximately $0.05-0.08/kWh. Pair solar with battery storage for best results. Existing NEM 2.0 customers are grandfathered for 20 years.
SCE NEM 3.0 in effect. Time-of-use rate optimization and battery storage strongly recommended to maximize savings under new export compensation structure.
SDG&E has the highest peak rates in California at $0.42/kWh. Solar plus storage maximizes savings by avoiding peak grid purchases and optimizing export timing.
NV Energy net metering program has reached capacity limits. New solar customers receive reduced export compensation. Battery storage increasingly important for maximizing value.
DTE Energy net metering cap reached. New residential solar customers are placed on the distributed generation program with avoided-cost export rates rather than full retail credit.
FirstEnergy Ohio approaching net metering capacity limits in some service territories. Contact utility for current availability before finalizing solar installation plans.
PSEG Long Island net metering capacity approaching limits. Interconnection queue times are increasing. Submit applications early to secure current compensation rates.

Hawaiian Electric traditional net metering is closed to new applicants. Smart Export and Customer Grid Supply programs available with lower export rates. Battery storage essential for new installations.
